To begin the process we define a stored procedure named clean_inactive_users that will handle the deletion logic safely.
This procedure starts a transaction to ensure data integrity before any rows are removed from the users table.
We select all user IDs where the status column equals Inactive to prepare them for the logging step.
Each selected user ID is then inserted into the status_log table with their current status and the current timestamp.
The status_log table structure must include user_id, status, and modification_date to track these historical changes accurately.
After logging the data we execute a delete command on the users table targeting the previously identified inactive records.
If any error occurs during the selection, insertion, or deletion steps the transaction will be rolled back immediately.
Error handling is implemented using a declare handler that catches any SQL exceptions and returns a descriptive message.
The procedure commits the transaction only if all operations complete without triggering any exceptions or warnings.
You can call this stored procedure whenever you need to clean up inactive records from your database system efficiently.
The total number of affected rows is calculated to verify the success of the deletion operation within the script.
This total count is returned to the caller so they can confirm how many records were successfully processed and removed.
The entire solution ensures that no data is lost without a corresponding entry in the status_log audit trail.
